summary: The vestibulocerebellum controls balance, the spinocerebellum controls posture and gait, and the pontocerebellum controls skilled voluntary movements.
The vestibulocerebellum controls voluntary movements, the spinocerebellum controls balance, and the pontocerebellum controls posture.
All three functional zones of the cerebellum have identical functions.
The intracerebellar nuclei from medial to lateral are Fastigial, Globosus, Emboliformis, and Dentate.
The intracerebellar nuclei from medial to lateral are Dentate, Emboliformis, Globosus, and Fastigial.
There are only two intracerebellar nuclei: Fastigial and Dentate.
The inferior cerebellar peduncle carries mainly input fibres including the posterior spinocerebellar tract.
The inferior cerebellar peduncle carries mainly output fibres to the motor cortex.
The inferior cerebellar peduncle is the largest of the three peduncles.
Cerebellar lesions cause ipsilateral deficits because cerebellar output crosses twice before reaching the motor neurons.
Cerebellar lesions cause contralateral deficits similar to cerebral cortex lesions.
Cerebellar output does not cross at any point in its pathway.
The middle cerebellar peduncle is the largest peduncle and carries corticopontocerebellar input fibres.
The superior cerebellar peduncle is the largest peduncle.
The middle cerebellar peduncle carries mainly output fibres.
Cerebellar dysfunction produces ataxia, intention tremor, dysmetria, and dysdiadochokinesia.
Cerebellar dysfunction produces resting tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia.
Cerebellar dysfunction produces spastic paralysis with hyperreflexia.
